payload.description: Implemented intelligent routing: smart complexity router, cost-based routing, provider budget caps+fallbacks (config-only), plus session affinity, cost tier, provider preference, budget awareness (new code in router.py). 43 new tests, 612 total passing.

payload.decision: Intelligent routing implemented as 7 features: 3 config-only (smart model, cost-based routing, provider budgets+fallbacks) + 4 Airlock code (session affinity, cost tiers, provider preference, budget awareness)
payload.rationale: Config-only features leverage LiteLLM built-ins with zero code. Code features give clients composable routing directives via metadata.airlock dict. Router runs in guardian between threat check and circuit breaker so routed model gets circuit-checked.
alternatives_rejected: Adding routing as a separate guardrail (would add latency and complexity vs inlining in guardian); putting routing logic in a LiteLLM callback (wrong lifecycle stage — needs to run pre_call before model selection is finalized)

payload.decision: Duplicate _infer_provider() in both monitor.py and router.py
payload.rationale: Both modules import state.py. Sharing a helper between them would create circular imports or require a new utils module for a 6-line function. Duplication is the simpler path.
alternatives_rejected: Shared helper in state.py (state shouldn't know about provider names); new utils module (over-engineering for a trivial function)

payload.decision: Router placement: inline in guardian between threat check (step 2) and circuit breaker (step 3)
payload.rationale: Routing must happen before circuit breaker so the breaker checks the ROUTED model, not the originally-requested one. If routing swaps to claude-haiku and haiku circuit is open, breaker correctly failovers haiku. After threat check because blocked clients shouldn't get routing logic.
alternatives_rejected: Before threat check (wasted compute on blocked clients); after circuit breaker (breaker would check wrong model); as a separate guardrail (extra overhead, ordering not guaranteed)

payload.summary: Client directives via metadata.airlock dict (session_id, cost_tier, prefer_provider). Priority order: session affinity > cost tier > provider preference > budget awareness. Session pins override all other directives on subsequent requests. New sessions apply directives 2-4 then pin result. StateStore extended with SessionRecord and ProviderSpend. Monitor tracks spend via response_cost kwarg. Config-only features: smart complexity router, cost-based routing strategy, provider budget caps, cross-provider fallbacks.

payload.approach: LiteLLM auto_router/complexity_router for smart model alias
payload.reason: LiteLLM auto-router requires semantic_router package, an embedding model, and route configs with utterances. It is NOT a lightweight complexity classifier — it is a full semantic routing system. Too heavy a dependency for a simple tier-based model selector.
payload.context: Error: auto_router_config_path or auto_router_config is required for auto-router deployments

payload.rule: LiteLLM auto_router requires: pip install semantic-router, auto_router_config (JSON routes with utterances), auto_router_default_model, and auto_router_embedding_model. Not a lightweight complexity classifier.
payload.reason: auto_router/complexity_router does not exist — the feature is auto_router/* backed by the semantic_router package which uses embedding similarity to classify queries into routes

payload.summary: auto_router/* models use semantic_router.SemanticRouter with embedding-based route matching. Required litellm_params: auto_router_config (JSON string with routes array, each route has name/description/utterances/score_threshold), auto_router_default_model, auto_router_embedding_model. Routes are matched by cosine similarity between user message embedding and route utterance embeddings. RouteChoice.name becomes the model name. Falls back to default_model if no route matches. The routelayer is lazily initialized on first request.

payload.decision: Native heuristic complexity classifier for model:smart — six weighted text features (token count, code blocks, reasoning keywords, multi-step indicators, vocab richness, sentence length), composite score 0-1 mapped to simple/moderate/complex tiers
payload.rationale: LiteLLM auto_router requires semantic_router package + embedding model — too heavy for hot path. Native classifier runs in ~50μs with no ML deps. Reuses existing cost tier infrastructure by injecting classified tier into airlock_meta.
alternatives_rejected: LiteLLM auto_router (requires semantic_router + embedding model, too heavy); ML-based classifier (adds deps, latency); simple keyword-only routing (too coarse, no scoring)

payload.summary: classify_complexity() in router.py uses 6 features weighted to sum 1.0: token_count(0.30) sigmoid 20-150 words, code_blocks(0.25) fenced/inline backticks, reasoning(0.20) 20-word frozenset saturates at 3 hits, multi_step(0.10) compiled regex for numbered lists + sequencing words, vocab_rich(0.10) unique/total ratio needs ≥10 words, sentence_len(0.05) avg words per sentence. Score <0.30=simple(low), 0.30-0.60=moderate(medium), ≥0.60=complex(high). Empty text fails to moderate. Thresholds configurable via AIRLOCK_SMART_THRESHOLDS env var. Smart classification runs before all other directives in apply_routing() and composes with session_id, prefer_provider, cost_tier.

payload.description: Implemented native complexity routing for model:smart. 25 new tests (637 total). Files: router.py (classifier + apply_routing integration), test_fast_router.py, config.yaml, templates/config.yaml, .env.example.

payload.decision: MCP guardrail dual-registration: all 7 existing guardrails register for both LLM and MCP hooks via supported_event_hooks lists + config mode lists
payload.rationale: One set of guardrail functions protects both LLM completions and MCP tool calls — enterprise value proposition. LiteLLM already has full MCP support with pre_mcp_call/during_mcp_call hooks.
alternatives_rejected: Building separate MCP guardrails (duplication), proxying MCP without guardrails (security gap)

payload.decision: Unified text extraction in extract.py dispatches by call_type and data keys — replaces 4 duplicate _extract_text functions
payload.rationale: MCP data shape differs from LLM (mcp_tool_name, mcp_arguments vs messages). Single extract_text(data, call_type) function handles both, includes tool name and argument values in scannable text. Eliminates duplication across keyword_guard, observer, guardian, semantic.
alternatives_rejected: Keeping separate extractors per guardrail (duplication), only scanning synthetic messages for MCP (misses tool name and arguments)

payload.decision: Guardian skips routing and circuit breaker for MCP calls but still applies threat detection, backoff, and priority scoring
payload.rationale: Routing and circuit breaker are model-specific concepts — MCP tool calls do not target LLM models. Threat detection and rate limiting still apply because a malicious client can abuse MCP tools just as they abuse LLM endpoints.
alternatives_rejected: Skipping all guardian checks for MCP (leaves threat gap), running routing for MCP (nonsensical — no model to route)

payload.summary: 8 guardrails dual-registered for LLM+MCP: PII guard scrubs mcp_arguments directly, keyword guard scans tool name + args, guardian skips routing/circuit-breaker for MCP, observer/orchestrator/enforcer pass call_type to collect_signals, semantic guard runs classifiers on MCP text, mcp_tool_guard (new) does allowlist/blocklist + arg sanitization. Config mode fields are lists. Enterprise logger adds call_type/mcp_tool_name/mcp_server_name. TUI flow shows Type column. POST has MCP group with 2 checks.

Post-call guardrail scanning LLM responses and MCP tool results for prompt injection, instruction override, data exfiltration, and suspicious tool calls. 4 regex categories with weighted composite scoring. Three hook paths: async_post_call_success_hook (non-streaming), async_post_call_streaming_iterator_hook (streaming, buffer-and-forward), async_post_mcp_tool_call_hook (MCP via self-registration). Default mode observe. Streaming cannot block retroactively — logs only. Metadata attached to data dict for enterprise logger pickup.

payload.decision: Response scanner uses regex-only detection with 4 weighted categories (injection 1.0, exfiltration 0.9, override 0.8, tool_call 0.7) and composite scoring
payload.rationale: Runs in critical path — must be microsecond-fast. No ML, no external APIs. Weighted categories let composite score reflect severity mix. Threshold configurable via env var.
alternatives_rejected: ML-based classifier (latency), external API scanner (dependency + latency), binary per-pattern blocking (no nuance in scoring)

payload.decision: Three separate hook methods for non-streaming, streaming, and MCP response paths
payload.rationale: LiteLLM dispatches post-call via guardrail system (post_call mode) for LLM, but MCP post-call goes through success_callback list. Streaming hook must be defined directly on the class (LiteLLM checks type.__dict__). Streaming cannot block retroactively since chunks already sent — detection is logged for slow analyzer.
alternatives_rejected: Single unified hook (impossible — different dispatch mechanisms), blocking streaming responses (chunks already sent to client)

payload.decision: Only attach airlock_response_scan metadata when detection occurs, not on clean responses
payload.rationale: Clean responses are the common case — attaching empty scan results on every response would bloat metadata and JSONL logs with noise. Enterprise logger already collects all airlock_* metadata keys.
alternatives_rejected: Always attaching metadata (noisy, bloats logs)

payload.summary: Three-agent review (reuse, quality, efficiency) found 16 issues. Fixed 8: dead if-guard, streaming buffer memory (full chunks→text-only), mode read twice, silent self-register exception, constant recomputation, shallow PII scrubbing for MCP args, duplicate recursive traversal (_check_arguments now uses _collect_strings), unbounded recursion (depth limit 20). Skipped 8 pre-existing issues: _extract_client_id duplication, _blocked_keywords duplication, CSV parsing 7x, CheckResult verbosity, self-registration boilerplate 3x, is_mcp_call inlined in enterprise_logger, call_mcp_tool as raw string, category names as raw strings.

payload.decision: Streaming response scanner accumulates only text strings, not full chunk objects
payload.rationale: Full chunk objects contain metadata beyond text content. For long streams, buffering doubles memory usage. Extracting delta.content inline during iteration eliminates retaining chunk objects entirely.
alternatives_rejected: Buffering full chunk objects then extracting text post-stream (original impl — wasteful memory)

payload.decision: _collect_strings has depth limit of 20 to guard against adversarial nested payloads
payload.rationale: Both _collect_strings (extract.py) and _check_arguments (mcp_tool_guard.py) recurse into nested dicts/lists. Maliciously crafted deeply nested MCP arguments could hit Python recursion limit. Depth 20 is generous for real data but prevents stack overflow.
alternatives_rejected: No depth limit (original impl — RecursionError on adversarial input)

payload.decision: _check_arguments reuses _collect_strings from extract.py instead of duplicating recursive traversal
payload.rationale: Both functions walked the same dict/list tree structure. If traversal needs to change (e.g. depth limit, tuple support), updating one place is sufficient. _check_arguments short-circuits on first dangerous match via generator iteration over _collect_strings results.
alternatives_rejected: Keeping separate recursive implementations in extract.py and mcp_tool_guard.py (duplication)

payload.decision: PII guard _scrub_mcp_arguments now recurses into nested dicts and lists
payload.rationale: extract.py _collect_strings and mcp_tool_guard.py _check_arguments already recurse into nested structures for detection and sanitization. PII guard only scrubbing top-level strings was a security gap — nested email like {config: {email: user@example.com}} would pass through unredacted.
alternatives_rejected: Shallow scrubbing of only top-level string values (original impl — missed nested PII)

payload.description: Added MCP visibility across all 5 TUI screens (Dashboard, Models, Settings, Logs, Flow), plus McpToolState in state layer and MCP tracking in monitor callbacks. 800 tests passing.

payload.summary: McpToolState dataclass in state.py tracks per-tool success/failure/latency with deque sliding windows, keyed by server/tool in StateStore. Monitor tracks MCP via call_type=='call_mcp_tool' or 'mcp_tool_name' in kwargs. Dashboard has MCP Gateway panel with traffic split MetricCard. Models has MCP tools DataTable. Settings has MCP tab with server config display and allowed/blocked tools inputs. Logs has type filter (All/LLM/MCP) and tool name filter, Type column. Flow has Server column and Tool Result tab.

payload.decision: McpToolState modeled after ModelState with deque sliding windows, not circuit breaker — MCP tools lack model-specific concepts like circuits and failover
payload.rationale: MCP tools are stateless invocations, not long-lived model endpoints. Success/failure/latency tracking is valuable but circuit-breaking is not meaningful for tool calls.
alternatives_rejected: Full ModelState clone with circuit breaker fields

payload.decision: Dashboard MCP indicator uses passive health derivation from traffic success/failure rates, not active probes
payload.rationale: MCP servers may use stdio transport (no URL to probe). Deriving health from observed error rates is consistent with how ModelState health works. Three states: warn (no traffic), error (>50% error rate on any tool), ok (tools active).
alternatives_rejected: Active health probes to MCP server endpoints

payload.decision: Models screen uses separate MCP tools DataTable below model detail, not merged into the model table
payload.rationale: Tools lack circuits, failover chains, and recovery timers — mixing them into the model table would leave columns empty and confuse concepts. Separate table with Tool/Server/Calls/Err%/Latency columns keeps concerns clean.
alternatives_rejected: Merging MCP tools into existing model DataTable with empty circuit/failover columns

payload.decision: StateStore traffic_split uses simple integer counters, not windowed counts
payload.rationale: Traffic split is a lifetime aggregate for the dashboard overview. Windowed counts would require deques and add complexity for a metric that is most useful as a cumulative ratio. Counters reset on process restart which is acceptable.
alternatives_rejected: Windowed deque-based traffic split tracking

payload.decision: MCP tool state keyed by server/tool compound key to distinguish same tool name across different MCP servers
payload.rationale: Two MCP servers could expose a tool with the same name (e.g. read_file). Compound key ensures per-server-per-tool tracking. Falls back to bare tool name when server_name is empty.
alternatives_rejected: Keying by tool name only (would merge metrics across servers)

payload.decision: MCP servers classified as remote/local/stdio with airlock_managed config subkey for local lifecycle management
payload.rationale: LiteLLM ignores unknown keys so extending mcp_servers entries is forward-compatible. Three categories map cleanly to health probe strategy and lifecycle capabilities.
alternatives_rejected: Separate airlock_local_mcp config section (splits config, harder to maintain); separate mcp_local.yaml file (adds file sprawl)

payload.decision: MCP server health probes use HTTP GET for URL-based servers and shutil.which for stdio binary existence
payload.rationale: HTTP GET is the simplest probe that confirms reachability. 4xx treated as healthy (server up but may need auth). stdio servers are ephemeral (LiteLLM spawns per-call) so binary existence is sufficient. Full MCP session health check too expensive for periodic probing.
alternatives_rejected: Full MCP client session probe for all server types (too slow for periodic health loop); no health probes (user explicitly requested this)

payload.decision: McpServerManager follows ProxyManager pattern with per-server ring buffers and reader threads, not a process supervisor
payload.rationale: ProxyManager is the proven pattern in the codebase for subprocess lifecycle. Per-server ring buffers (500 lines) + output queues enable TUI console streaming. No auto-restart daemon — user explicitly ruled out process supervisor.
alternatives_rejected: Process supervisor with auto-restart (out of scope); single shared ring buffer across all servers (loses per-server context)

payload.summary: configure_logging() in airlock/cli/main.py sets up file handler (DEBUG+ to logs/airlock-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.log) and stderr handler (WARNING+) on the airlock root logger. All 20 child loggers inherit. Idempotent guard prevents duplicate handlers. Called after arg parse, only for real subcommands (not --help). Uses AIRLOCK_LOG_DIR env var (default ./logs).

LiteLLM validates MCP server names cannot contain hyphens (raises Exception in litellm/proxy/_experimental/mcp_server/utils.py:validate_mcp_server_name). Use underscores instead. Discovered when ado-mcp caused startup failure.

payload.decision: configure_logging() called after argparse dispatch check, not at top of main()
payload.rationale: Prevents creating log files and directories for --help or no-args invocations. Only real subcommands need logging.
alternatives_rejected: Calling configure_logging() unconditionally after load_dotenv() — creates spurious log files on --help

payload.decision: Idempotent handler guard in configure_logging() — early return if airlock logger already has handlers
payload.rationale: main() is called ~12 times in tests. Without the guard, each call accumulates duplicate file and stderr handlers causing duplicate log lines and leaked file descriptors.
alternatives_rejected: No idempotency guard — handlers accumulate on repeated main() calls in tests

payload.rule: LiteLLM MCP server names cannot contain hyphens
payload.reason: validate_mcp_server_name() in LiteLLM raises Exception on hyphen. Use underscores.

payload.rule: os.environ/ refs in mcp_servers config are resolved from Airlock process env at config load time, not from the MCP server project .env
payload.reason: LiteLLM reads os.environ/ values during YAML config parsing. The child MCP process .env is never consulted. All referenced vars must exist in Airlock .env or shell.

payload.rule: LiteLLM MCP stdio servers MUST set transport: stdio in config — LiteLLM defaults to http transport, causing tool listing to fail
payload.reason: LiteLLM load_servers_from_config defaults transport to MCPTransport.http. Without explicit stdio, the MCP client tries HTTP against an empty URL and gets cancelled.

payload.decision: Tavily exposed as custom LiteLLM provider (CustomLLM subclass) rather than MCP server — wraps TavilyClient.search() into chat completion response format
payload.rationale: User requested it as a LiteLLM model. CustomLLM provider integrates with LiteLLM routing, guardrails, and logging natively. Config uses custom_provider_map in litellm_settings.
alternatives_rejected: MCP stdio server for Tavily (would work but doesn't integrate with model routing); direct API proxy (bypasses guardrails)

payload.decision: NewsCatcher wrapped as stdio MCP server using mcp SDK Server class with two tools (newscatcher_search, newscatcher_search_quick)
payload.rationale: NewsCatcher CatchAll is a jobs-based async API (2-10 min). MCP server is the right abstraction for tool calls. Quick variant with 60s timeout for interactive use. Mirrors margie's implementation patterns (lazy client, poll loop, snippet extraction).
alternatives_rejected: LiteLLM custom model provider (search isn't a chat completion); blocking synchronous wrapper (jobs take minutes)

payload.decision: ModelAliasTable uses multi-signal scoring (containment, version-stripped comparison, token overlap, provider affinity) not Levenshtein/Jaro-Winkler string distance
payload.rationale: Model names have semantic structure (provider-family-version) that pure string distance ignores. Token overlap respects this structure. Cross-provider penalty prevents misroutes (claude-* never matches gpt-*). Version stripping handles the most common mismatch pattern. Scoring runs in ~10μs per entry.
alternatives_rejected: Levenshtein distance (too many false positives across providers); Jaro-Winkler only (rewards prefix similarity but doesn't understand model name semantics); LiteLLM wildcard routing (fragile, doesn't handle fuzzy name resolution)

payload.decision: MCP servers use project-specific venv binaries instead of uv run
payload.rationale: uv run resolves against Airlock's venv where sibling projects (ado-mcp, armada) are not installed. Pointing command to each project's own .venv/bin/<entry-point> ensures correct package resolution without coupling projects as dependencies.
alternatives_rejected: Adding sibling projects as path dependencies to airlock pyproject.toml (couples unrelated projects); installing packages into airlock venv (version conflicts, heavy deps like torch)

payload.rule: MCP stdio server commands must use absolute paths to project-specific venv binaries, not bare uv run
payload.reason: uv run resolves against the current project venv. Sibling MCP server projects have their own venvs with their own dependencies. Bare uv run causes ModuleNotFoundError.

payload.approach: armada-mcp as active MCP server in Airlock config
payload.reason: armada-mcp hangs during MCP initialize handshake — no stdout response, no stderr. Server starts but never completes protocol init. Issue is in armada server code, not Airlock config.
payload.context: Tested with JSON-RPC initialize request piped to stdin, 10s timeout, zero output on both stdout and stderr

payload.summary: ado-mcp is a sibling project at /home/coreyt/projects/ado-mcp. Uses FastMCP 3.0.2, stdio transport. Entry point: .venv/bin/ado-mcp. Supports --profile core|full, --config, --org-url, --pat. Requires ADO_ORG_URL and ADO_PAT env vars. MCP handshake verified working. Reports 3.0.2 server version, supports tools (listChanged), resources, prompts.

payload.text: Fixed load_dotenv path resolution: bare load_dotenv() uses find_dotenv() which walks from CWD — fails when hooks/CLI invoked from different dirs. Changed to explicit Path(__file__)-relative .env path in _common.py, main.py, proxy.py
payload.author: human

payload.text: Fixed 0.0.0.0 bind address used as connect address: on WSL2, connecting to 0.0.0.0 hangs/times out. All health probes now remap 0.0.0.0 to 127.0.0.1 for actual connection. Applied in _common.py, dashboard.py, status_cmd.py, dogfood_cmd.py
payload.author: human

payload.text: Added ?client= query param to all health probes for log attribution: hook-session-start, cli-status, cli-dogfood, tui-dashboard. Enables identifying source of unauthenticated requests in proxy logs
payload.author: human

payload.text: Fixed ANSI rendering in TUI console: LiteLLM subprocess emits ANSI escape codes. RichLog.write(str) treats strings as Rich markup, not ANSI — causes stray box characters. Fixed by using Text.from_ansi(line) in dashboard.py and mcp_servers.py
payload.author: human

payload.text: Fixed dashboard model table never updating: TUI and LiteLLM proxy run as separate processes with separate StateStore singletons. Added JSONL log tailing in app.py — tail_jsonl() reads enterprise logger output and populates TUI-side StateStore via ingest_jsonl_record()
payload.author: human

payload.text: Fixed proxy status indicator stuck on Starting: Textual @work(thread=True) widget mutations from background threads don't reliably trigger repaints. Changed _check_health to use self.app.call_from_thread(_update_ui) to marshal UI updates to main thread
payload.author: human

payload.rule: Textual @work(thread=True) handlers must use self.app.call_from_thread() for any widget mutations — direct widget access from background threads does not reliably trigger repaints
payload.reason: Dashboard health check and state refresh widgets were not updating because mutations from worker threads were not marshaled to the UI thread

payload.rule: Health probes must remap 0.0.0.0 to 127.0.0.1 before connecting — 0.0.0.0 is a bind address, not connectable on WSL2
payload.reason: Connecting to 0.0.0.0 hangs/times out on WSL2. All probe sites (hooks, CLI, TUI) must translate to loopback.

payload.rule: load_dotenv() must use explicit Path(__file__)-relative path to .env, not bare load_dotenv() which uses find_dotenv() from CWD
payload.reason: Hooks spawned as separate processes and CLI invoked from different directories fail to find .env when using bare load_dotenv()

payload.decision: Cross-process state sync via JSONL log tailing — TUI tails enterprise logger JSONL file to populate its own StateStore
payload.rationale: TUI and LiteLLM proxy run as separate processes with separate StateStore singletons. JSONL is already written by enterprise_logger callback. Tailing avoids IPC complexity.
alternatives_rejected: Shared memory StateStore (requires multiprocessing Manager, complex); Redis/SQLite intermediary (adds dependency); direct IPC pipe (couples processes)

payload.decision: ANSI console output uses Text.from_ansi() not raw string write to RichLog
payload.rationale: LiteLLM subprocess emits ANSI escape codes. RichLog.write(str) interprets strings as Rich markup, causing rendering artifacts (stray box characters). Text.from_ansi() properly converts ANSI sequences to Rich Text objects.
alternatives_rejected: Stripping ANSI codes entirely (loses color information); writing raw bytes (RichLog expects Text/str)

payload.summary: Textual @work(thread=True) runs code in a background thread. Widget mutations (setting labels, updating status, clearing tables) from these threads do NOT reliably trigger screen repaints. Must use self.app.call_from_thread(callback) to marshal UI updates to the main thread. This pattern is used in dashboard _check_health and _refresh_state.

payload.decision: Capture AIRLOCK_CLIENT from request headers and send it on Airlock-owned health probes via a shared helper
payload.rationale: Proxy logs need per-request client attribution instead of relying on the proxy process environment; health probes should carry the same identity signal.
alternatives_rejected: Rely only on process env in callbacks; infer probe source only from query params

payload.description: Added AIRLOCK_CLIENT propagation for Airlock-owned health probes and request-side capture in logging/monitoring with targeted test coverage.

payload.decision: Standardize Airlock integration in sibling projects around AIRLOCK_HOST, AIRLOCK_PORT, AIRLOCK_MASTER_KEY, and AIRLOCK_CLIENT with project-local fallbacks only where needed
payload.rationale: The user wants a single env contract across projects when they use the Airlock/LiteLLM router; current integrations are inconsistent and often omit auth or client attribution.
alternatives_rejected: Leave project-specific router env vars as primary interface; patch only one repository

payload.description: Standardized Airlock request attribution on X-Airlock-Client only across Airlock and patched sibling project router clients; updated tests accordingly.

payload.decision: Implement provider protection inside the existing fast state/guardian/monitor path with client-provider quarantine and provider-wide escalation
payload.rationale: This reuses the current fast subsystem instead of inventing a separate rate-limit layer, lets no_client be tracked as a first-class client bucket, and keeps circuit-break, routing, logging, and TUI driven from one shared store.
alternatives_rejected: A separate standalone provider-throttle subsystem outside fast state and the current model-only circuit breaker without client/provider awareness

payload.description: Implemented client/provider quarantine, no_client normalization, pinned-model 429 blocking, unpinned model override metadata, clearer provider/client TUI state, and verified fast/integration tests.

Added a reusable live proxy round-trip harness base class and live matrix tests for every configured model alias plus smart override behavior, with assertions against response headers and Airlock JSON logs.

payload.topic: live harness auth failure after .env export
payload.summary: Rerunning tests/harness/test_phase5_live_matrix.py with AIRLOCK_* loaded from .env changed the failure from explicit no_db_connection auth fallback to a 400 ProxyException raised from LiteLLM user_api_key_auth.py line 982 before provider call. Evidence indicates the harness is sending AIRLOCK_MASTER_KEY from .env, but the running LiteLLM proxy process was likely started without the same AIRLOCK_MASTER_KEY, so the bearer token is not recognized as the configured master key.

payload.description: Updated TUI lifecycle so quitting airlock tui does not stop the LiteLLM proxy; proxy subprocess now starts in a detached session and TUI unmount no longer calls proxy_manager.stop().

payload.decision: Prefer X-Airlock-Client for request metadata over auth-derived client IDs
payload.rationale: Live harness proved provider calls worked but structured request metadata was still key-scoped, which breaks client attribution and provider protection logic.
alternatives_rejected: Leave airlock_request.client_id auth-derived and relax the harness assertions

payload.summary: After restarting the detached proxy, the live matrix passed Anthropic, OpenAI, gemini-flash, and gemini-flash-lite. The prior client attribution mismatch was resolved. The next failure is gemini-pro returning 200 with assistant content null and finish_reason length, which breaks a too-strict harness assertion.

The live harness now distinguishes textful success from valid empty success. For gemini-pro, baseline requests can return 200 with content=null and text_tokens=0. Adding reasoning_effort=disable (or thinking budget 0) produces message.content text reliably.

payload.description: Implement Gemini semantic layer and response visibility across Airlock callbacks, TUI, and harness
Implemented Gemini semantic request mapping, Gemini response headers/logging, TUI/provider visibility, and targeted live harness verification

payload.decision: Pinned requests now stamp disable_fallbacks and zero retries before leaving Airlock
payload.rationale: LiteLLM was still performing post-failure fallback on pinned requests after real OpenAI quota errors, bypassing Airlock policy.
alternatives_rejected: Rely on empty fallbacks lists or only adjust Airlock pre-call behavior without setting downstream router flags

payload.description: Verify OpenAI rate-limit evidence and enforce no fallback for pinned requests

payload.decision: Unify monitor client IDs with guardian for provider quarantine state
payload.rationale: The monitor stored Airlock-attributed clients as airlock:<client> while guardian checked plain X-Airlock-Client values, so repeated pinned requests were never blocked by the recorded quarantine.
alternatives_rejected: Leave quarantine state auth-key-scoped or under a separate prefixed client namespace.

payload.description: Verified real OpenAI quota failures and enforced no LiteLLM fallback for pinned requests, including matching client-provider quarantine state.

payload.decision: Emit structured JSON records directly from guardian for pre-call blocked_429 failures
payload.rationale: Provider-protection blocks happen before LiteLLM failure callbacks, so relying on the normal logger path leaves no JSONL record for those Airlock-generated 429s.
alternatives_rejected: Assume LiteLLM will always log pre-call guardrail exceptions on the normal failure callback path.

payload.decision: TUI exit stops the proxy by default; only --daemon keeps it running after exit
payload.rationale: The user expects quitting the dashboard to also stop routing unless daemonization is explicitly requested, so detaching the proxy must be opt-in rather than the default.
alternatives_rejected: Always detach the proxy when started from the TUI, or infer daemon behavior implicitly from --start.

payload.decision: Enrich LiteLLM OpenAPI in place and add a linked Airlock conceptual docs page at /airlock/docs
payload.rationale: Airlock changes are endpoint-level semantic overlays on the existing LiteLLM API surface, so the canonical client contract should stay on the existing Swagger/ReDoc schema while a separate conceptual page handles routing, provider protection, and Gemini behavior that OpenAPI expresses poorly.
alternatives_rejected: Separate standalone OpenAPI surface or leaving LiteLLM docs unmodified with only external prose docs.

payload.decision: Remove sidecar FastAPI reverse proxy; run LiteLLM directly on the public port
payload.rationale: LiteLLM 1.82.0 natively returns model_name aliases from config in GET /v1/models, which was the only differentiated value the sidecar provided. Live provider discovery is now a startup utility (logging only). Removing the sidecar eliminates one proxy hop, the internal port dance, and a separate uvicorn process without losing any capability.
alternatives_rejected: Keep sidecar for future request-inspection hooks — LiteLLM pre_call guardrails already cover this without an extra hop.
Upgrade to a newer LiteLLM version for alias support — 1.82.0 already supports it.

payload.description: Removed airlock/sidecar.py and FastAPI reverse proxy layer. proxy.py now uses subprocess.call to run LiteLLM directly. models_catalog.py trimmed to discovery-only utility. tests/test_sidecar.py deleted; test_proxy.py and test_models_catalog.py updated.

payload.decision: observer._extract_client_id uses normalize_client_id(None) fallback, not hardcoded "unknown"
payload.rationale: Observer was returning "unknown" for no-client requests while guardian returned "no_client". These hit different ClientState buckets, so observer threat reads were disconnected from guardian threat writes entirely. Aligning to normalize_client_id(None) ensures the same bucket is used throughout the pipeline.
alternatives_rejected: Change guardian to return "unknown" instead — normalize_client_id(None) is the canonical fallback in state.py.

payload.decision: _normalize_failure third branch labeled "pre_call" not "eval"
payload.rationale: The elif branch was dead code: the condition was identical to the if above it, so both branches produced category="eval". Requests that fail before any provider interaction but are not eval requests should be labeled "pre_call" to allow downstream filtering by failure category.
alternatives_rejected: Drop the third branch entirely and default to "provider" — "pre_call" is semantically distinct from provider-side failures.

payload.decision: Gemini outcome tracking in monitor is unconditional on provider cost, not gated behind cost==0
payload.rationale: record_gemini_outcome was placed in the zero-cost else branch. LiteLLM computes Gemini costs from token counts so cost>0 is the normal production path; the else branch almost never fires. Moving the gemini outcome block outside the cost conditional makes TUI Gemini stats and routing decisions work in practice.
alternatives_rejected: Gate on cost==0 as a free-tier indicator — Gemini outcome shape is useful regardless of cost tier.

payload.decision: threat_detector decay uses last_request_times[-1] (most recent prior request) not [-2]
payload.rationale: The decay formula computes 0.95^elapsed to blend the accumulated threat score. Using [-2] measured the gap between the two most recent previous requests, not the idle time before the current request — giving a larger elapsed value and causing the score to over-decay (threats dissipated faster than intended). [-1] is the correct index since assess_threat runs pre-call before the current timestamp is recorded.
alternatives_rejected: Keep [-2] — the comment explicitly said "time since previous request" and the test suite confirmed the wrong semantics.

payload.topic: LiteLLM /v1/models returns config alias names natively since 1.82.0
payload.summary: get_model_names() returns deployment["model_name"] (the alias) for all configured deployments. owned_by is hardcoded to "openai" for all entries. Provider-pinned model IDs (e.g. anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet) are not returned but no Airlock client depends on them. This made the sidecar catalog builder redundant.

payload.topic: LiteLLM callback pre_call / during_call / post_call execution order
payload.summary: assess_threat runs in pre_call_hook before the request is sent to the provider. client.request_times is not yet updated at that point — it gets updated by monitor.log_success_event / log_failure_event after the call completes. Any code in threat_detector that reads request_times sees only previous requests, not the current one.
